title: The autoionization of water and Kᵥᵥ
definition: Water is both a weak acid and a weak base to itself: two molecules trade a proton, $2\,\mathrm{H_2O} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H_3O^+} + \mathrm{OH^-}$, so even pure water carries a trace of ions. Their product is the **ion-product constant of water**, $K_w = [\mathrm{H^+}][\mathrm{OH^-}] = 1.0\times10^{-14}$ at 25 °C. This is the **bridge between acids and bases**: whatever raises $[\mathrm{H^+}]$ must lower $[\mathrm{OH^-}]$ to keep the product at $K_w$, and vice versa. Taking $-\log_{10}$ of both sides turns the product into a sum, $\mathrm{pH} + \mathrm{pOH} = \mathrm{p}K_w = 14.00$ — so measuring a weak **base**'s $[\mathrm{OH^-}]$ (from its $K_b$ equilibrium) immediately gives its pH: $\mathrm{pH} = 14.00 - \mathrm{pOH}$. In pure water $[\mathrm{H^+}] = [\mathrm{OH^-}] = 10^{-7}\,\text{M}$, i.e. pH 7 — the definition of neutral.
